title: Daily Temperatures
description: Find number of days until a warmer temperature for each day

examples.explanation: Day 0 (30°): Check day 1 (40°) → 40>30 → answer[0]=1-0=1. Day 1 (40°): Check day 2 (50°) → 50>40 → answer[1]=2-1=1. Day 2 (50°): Check day 3 (60°) → 60>50 → answer[2]=3-2=1. Day 3 (60°): No subsequent days → answer[3]=0.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each day, iterate through subsequent days to find the first warmer temperature. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Solution for Daily Temperatures
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the O(n²) brute force approach to find days until warmer temperature for each day.

explanation.prose: The brute force approach solves the Daily Temperatures problem by directly implementing the problem's core requirement: for each day, we need to find how many days we must wait until a warmer temperature occurs. The intuition is straightforward - we examine each day in sequence, then look ahead through all subsequent days to find the first occurrence of a higher temperature. This approach works because it directly implements the problem statement without any optimization. The algorithm proceeds as follows: 1) Initialize an answer array of zeros with the same length as the input temperatures. 2) For each day i from 0 to n-1: a) For each subsequent day j from i+1 to n-1: b) If temperature[j] > temperature[i], set answer[i] = j-i and break the inner loop. 3) Return the answer array. This connects to the full problem by being the complete, unoptimized solution that directly solves the original problem statement. The real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def dailyTemperatures(temperatures): n = len(temperatures); answer = [0]*n; for i in range(n): for j in range(i+1, n): if temperatures[j] > temperatures[i]: answer[i] = j-i; break; return answer.

timeComplexity.note: because for each of n days, we potentially examine up to n subsequent days in the worst case (when temperatures are decreasing)

spaceComplexity.note: excluding the output array, as we only use a constant amount of extra space

commonMistakes: Forgetting to break after finding the first warmer day, which would incorrectly count multiple warmer days
Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries
Not handling edge cases like empty input or single-day input

examples.trace: Day 0 (30°): Check day 1 (40°) → 40>30 → answer[0]=1-0=1. Day 1 (40°): Check day 2 (50°) → 50>40 → answer[1]=2-1=1. Day 2 (50°): Check day 3 (60°) → 60>50 → answer[2]=3-2=1. Day 3 (60°): No subsequent days → answer[3]=0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding First Warmer Day for a Given Day
ladders.desc: Implement the core inner loop that finds the first warmer temperature after a specific day.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine represents the most critical component of the brute force approach: given a specific day index i and the temperatures array, find the index of the first subsequent day with a higher temperature. The intuition is that we need to sequentially examine days after i until we encounter a temperature greater than temperature[i]. This works because we're implementing the fundamental search operation required by the problem. The algorithm: 1) Receive parameters: temperatures array and current day index i. 2) Initialize result = 0 (meaning no warmer day found). 3) For j from i+1 to end of array: a) If temperatures[j] > temperatures[i], set result = j-i and break. 4) Return result.

connectsTo.reason: by being the inner loop that gets called for each day in the complete solution

timeComplexity.note: in worst case when we examine all subsequent days

commonMistakes: Starting j from i instead of i+1 (would compare day with itself)
Not breaking after finding first warmer day
Returning j instead of j-i (wrong units)
explanation.codePattern: def findWarmerDay(temperatures, i): n = len(temperatures); for j in range(i+1, n): if temperatures[j] > temperatures[i]: return j-i; return 0

examples.trace: Day i=2 has temperature 75. Check j=3: 71≤75. j=4: 69≤75. j=5: 72≤75. j=6: 76>75 → Found at j=6. Return j-i = 6-2 = 4.
Day i=1 has temperature 40. Check j=2: 50>40 → Found at j=2. Return j-i = 2-1 = 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sequential Comparison Loop
ladders.desc: Implement a loop that compares each element with subsequent elements until a condition is met.

explanation.prose: This core logic represents the fundamental pattern of searching through an array by comparing a reference element with each subsequent element until a condition is satisfied. The intuition is that many problems require examining elements in relation to their successors, and this pattern of 'compare and advance' is a fundamental algorithmic building block. This works by maintaining a current position and systematically checking each following element. The algorithm: 1) Take an array and a starting index. 2) For each index from start+1 to end: a) Compare current element with reference element. b) If comparison condition is met, return the result. 3) If loop completes without meeting condition, return default value.

connectsTo.reason: by being the exact pattern used in findWarmerDay, where the condition is 'temperature > reference temperature'

timeComplexity.note: for scanning potentially all subsequent elements

commonMistakes: Infinite loops from incorrect increment
Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries
Not handling the 'not found' case properly
explanation.codePattern: def sequentialCompare(arr, start_idx): ref = arr[start_idx]; for i in range(start_idx+1, len(arr)): if arr[i] > ref: return i-start_idx; return 0

examples.trace: Reference element = arr[1] = 3. Check i=2: arr[2]=7 > 3 → Condition met. Return i-start_idx = 2-1 = 1? Wait, correction: i=2, start_idx=1, so difference is 1. Actually the function should return the index difference. Let me trace properly: start_idx=1, ref=3. i=2: arr[2]=7>3 → return 2-1=1. But the example says output 2? Let me check: The problem expects days until warmer, which is index difference. For temperatures, if day 1 (index 1) is 3° and day 2 (index 2) is 7°, that's 1 day later (2-1=1). The example seems wrong. Let me use correct example: For arr=[5,3,7,2,8], start_idx=1, ref=3. i=2: 7>3 → return 1 (index difference). Actually the output should be 1, not 2. I'll correct: output should be 1.
Reference element = arr[0] = 10. Check i=1: 9≤10. i=2: 8≤10. i=3: 7≤10. i=4: 6≤10. Loop completes without finding element >10. Return default 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Nested Loop Pattern for Array Processing
ladders.desc: Implement the pattern of processing each array element by examining all subsequent elements.

explanation.prose: This building block introduces the nested loop pattern where for each element in an array, we examine all elements that come after it. The intuition is that many algorithms require comparing each element with every other element that follows it, creating an O(n²) pattern. This works by using an outer loop to iterate through each position and an inner loop to iterate through all subsequent positions. The algorithm: 1) Initialize result array of same length as input, filled with zeros. 2) For i from 0 to n-1 (outer loop): a) For j from i+1 to n-1 (inner loop): b) Perform some operation comparing element i and element j. c) If condition met, update result[i] and break inner loop. 3) Return result array.

connectsTo.reason: by showing how the sequential comparison gets applied to every element in the array

commonMistakes: Using j from 0 instead of i+1 (wasteful re-comparisons)
Not breaking inner loop when condition met (if appropriate)
Index confusion between i and j
explanation.codePattern: def processArray(arr): n = len(arr); result = [0]*n; for i in range(n): for j in range(i+1, n): if arr[j] > arr[i]: result[i] = j-i; break; return result

examples.trace: Initialize result=[0,0,0,0]. i=0 (3): j=1: 1≤3; j=2: 4>3 → result[0]=2-0=2, break. i=1 (1): j=2: 4>1 → result[1]=2-1=1, break. i=2 (4): j=3: 2≤4 → no break, loop ends → result[2]=0. i=3 (2): no j (j starts at 4≥n) → result[3]=0. Final result=[2,1,0,0].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Array Elements with Conditional Break
ladders.desc: Implement element comparison in a loop with early exit when condition is met.

explanation.prose: This basic operation focuses on the fundamental programming construct of comparing elements in an array and exiting a loop early when a specific condition is satisfied. The intuition is that many search operations require examining elements sequentially but stopping as soon as we find what we're looking for, which is more efficient than examining all elements. This works by iterating through array indices and using a conditional check with a break statement. The algorithm: 1) Initialize a variable to track result (default 0). 2) For index from start to end: a) Access array element at current index. b) Compare with reference value. c) If condition true, set result and break loop. 3) Return result.

connectsTo.reason: by being the inner loop body that gets executed within the nested loop structure

timeComplexity.note: but often better with early exit

commonMistakes: Forgetting to initialize result variable
Using wrong comparison operator
Placing break in wrong location
explanation.codePattern: def findFirstGreater(arr, start, ref_value): result = 0; for i in range(start, len(arr)): if arr[i] > ref_value: result = i-start; break; return result

examples.trace: Initialize result=0. i=1: arr[1]=3 ≤3? Actually 3 is not >3, so condition false. i=2: arr[2]=7>3 → condition true. Set result = i-start = 2-1 = 1. Break loop. Return 1.
Initialize result=0. i=0: arr[0]=10≤25. i=1: arr[1]=20≤25. i=2: arr[2]=30>25 → condition true. Set result = i-start = 2-0 = 2. Break loop. Return 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Traversal and Element Access
ladders.desc: Basic operations of iterating through an array and accessing elements by index.

explanation.prose: This foundation concept covers the most fundamental operations needed for the brute force approach: how to traverse an array and access its elements. The intuition is that all array algorithms build upon these basic operations of moving through the array sequentially and reading values at specific positions. This works because arrays provide constant-time access to elements using indices. The algorithm: 1) Get the length of the array. 2) Use a loop to iterate from 0 to length-1. 3) In each iteration, access the array element at the current index. 4) Optionally perform simple operations with the element.

connectsTo.reason: by providing the basic loop structure that gets enhanced with comparisons and conditional breaks

commonMistakes: Off-by-one errors (using <= instead of < for array bounds)
Accessing indices outside array bounds
Confusing array values with indices
explanation.codePattern: def traverseArray(arr): for i in range(len(arr)): current_element = arr[i]; # Do something with current_element

examples.trace: Array length = 4. i=0: access arr[0]=10. i=1: access arr[1]=20. i=2: access arr[2]=30. i=3: access arr[3]=40. End of loop.
Array length = 1. i=0: access arr[0]=5. End of loop.

approaches.name: Stack O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a monotonic decreasing stack to store indices of temperatures. When a warmer temperature is found, pop from the stack and compute the day difference. This efficiently processes each element once.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Daily Temperatures with Monotonic Stack
ladders.desc: Complete solution using a monotonic decreasing stack to find days until warmer temperature for each day.

explanation.prose: The Daily Temperatures problem requires finding, for each day's temperature, how many days you must wait until a warmer temperature occurs. The brute force approach would be O(n²) by checking each subsequent day. The optimal O(n) solution uses a monotonic decreasing stack to store indices of temperatures not yet resolved. The intuition is that when we encounter a temperature higher than the temperature at the top of the stack, we've found a warmer day for that stacked index. We pop it, compute the day difference (current index - stacked index), and store it in the result array. This works because the stack maintains temperatures in decreasing order, so when we find a warmer temperature, it's the first warmer day for all popped indices. The algorithm processes each temperature once: either pushing it onto the stack or using it to resolve previous temperatures. This approach efficiently finds the next greater element for each index, a pattern applicable to similar problems.

timeComplexity.note: since each index is pushed and popped at most once

spaceComplexity.note: for the stack and result array

commonMistakes: incorrect index arithmetic
not handling the stack properly when empty
or misunderstanding the monotonic property. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the key subroutine of popping
computing differences when a warmer temperature is found

examples.trace:
Initialize result = [0,0,0,0,0,0,0,0], stack = [].
Index 0 (73): stack empty, push 0 → stack = [0].
Index 1 (74): 74 > 73 (temp at stack top index 0). Pop 0, result[0] = 1-0 = 1 → result = [1,0,0,0,0,0,0,0]. Stack empty, push 1 → stack = [1].
Index 2 (75): 75 > 74 (temp at index 1). Pop 1, result[1] = 2-1 = 1 → result = [1,1,0,0,0,0,0,0]. Stack empty, push 2 → stack = [2].
Index 3 (71): 71 <= 75, push 3 → stack = [2,3].
Index 4 (69): 69 <= 71, push 4 → stack = [2,3,4].
Index 5 (72): 72 > 69 (temp at index 4). Pop 4, result[4] = 5-4 = 1 → result = [1,1,0,0,1,0,0,0]. Stack = [2,3]. 72 > 71 (temp at index 3). Pop 3, result[3] = 5-3 = 2 → result = [1,1,0,2,1,0,0,0]. Stack = [2]. 72 <= 75, push 5 → stack = [2,5].
Index 6 (76): 76 > 72 (temp at index 5). Pop 5, result[5] = 6-5 = 1 → result = [1,1,0,2,1,1,0,0]. Stack = [2]. 76 > 75 (temp at index 2). Pop 2, result[2] = 6-2 = 4 → result = [1,1,4,2,1,1,0,0]. Stack empty, push 6 → stack = [6].
Index 7 (73): 73 <= 76, push 7 → stack = [6,7].
End: result = [1,1,4,2,1,1,0,0].

Initialize result = [0,0,0,0], stack = [].
Index 0 (30): stack empty, push 0 → stack = [0].
Index 1 (40): 40 > 30. Pop 0, result[0] = 1-0 = 1 → result = [1,0,0,0]. Stack empty, push 1 → stack = [1].
Index 2 (50): 50 > 40. Pop 1, result[1] = 2-1 = 1 → result = [1,1,0,0]. Stack empty, push 2 → stack = [2].
Index 3 (60): 60 > 50. Pop 2, result[2] = 3-2 = 1 → result = [1,1,1,0]. Stack empty, push 3 → stack = [3].
End: result = [1,1,1,0].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Popping Stack on Warmer Temperature
ladders.desc: The core operation of popping indices from stack when a warmer temperature is found and computing day differences.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine is the heart of the monotonic stack solution. Given a current temperature and its index, and a stack storing indices of previous temperatures in decreasing order, we repeatedly check if the current temperature is greater than the temperature at the index on top of the stack. If it is, we pop that index from the stack, compute the day difference as current index - popped index, and store this value in the result array at the popped index position. This resolves that previous day's wait for a warmer temperature. We continue popping while the stack is not empty and the current temperature is greater than the temperature at the top index. The intuition is that the stack maintains a sequence of temperatures waiting for a warmer day, and the current temperature might be the first warmer day for multiple previous days if it's high enough. This operation ensures each previous day gets the smallest possible wait time. Time complexity for this subroutine in isolation is O(k) where k is the number of pops, but amortized over the full algorithm it's O(1) per element. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of resolving waiting days.

connectsTo.reason: This subroutine is called for each temperature in the main loop

spaceComplexity.note: extra beyond the stack and result

commonMistakes: not using a while loop (only popping once)
incorrect index arithmetic
or accessing temperatures with wrong indices. The pseudocode pattern: while stack not empty
temperatures[current_index] > temperatures[stack[-1]]: popped_index = stack.pop(); result[popped_index] = current_index - popped_index

examples.input: Current index: 5, temperature: 72, stack: [2,3,4] (temps: index2=75, index3=71, index4=69), result: [1,1,0,0,0,0,0,0]
examples.trace:
Start: current_index=5, temp=72, stack=[2,3,4], result=[1,1,0,0,0,0,0,0].
Check stack top index=4, temp at index4=69. 72 > 69 → true. Pop 4, result[4] = 5-4 = 1 → result=[1,1,0,0,1,0,0,0]. Stack=[2,3].
Check stack top index=3, temp at index3=71. 72 > 71 → true. Pop 3, result[3] = 5-3 = 2 → result=[1,1,0,2,1,0,0,0]. Stack=[2].
Check stack top index=2, temp at index2=75. 72 > 75 → false. Stop. Final stack=[2], result=[1,1,0,2,1,0,0,0].

examples.input: Current index: 3, temperature: 60, stack: [0,1,2] (temps: index0=30, index1=40, index2=50), result: [0,0,0,0]
examples.trace:
Start: current_index=3, temp=60, stack=[0,1,2], result=[0,0,0,0].
Check stack top index=2, temp at index2=50. 60 > 50 → true. Pop 2, result[2] = 3-2 = 1 → result=[0,0,1,0]. Stack=[0,1].
Check stack top index=1, temp at index1=40. 60 > 40 → true. Pop 1, result[1] = 3-1 = 1 → result=[0,1,1,0]. Stack=[0].
Check stack top index=0, temp at index0=30. 60 > 30 → true. Pop 0, result[0] = 3-0 = 1 → result=[1,1,1,0]. Stack=[].
Stack empty, stop. Final stack=[], result=[1,1,1,0].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Monotonic Decreasing Stack
ladders.desc: The technique of keeping a stack where temperatures are in decreasing order from bottom to top.

explanation.prose: A monotonic decreasing stack is a stack where elements (here indices of temperatures) are maintained such that the corresponding temperatures are in strictly decreasing order from bottom to top. This means the temperature at the bottom index is the highest, and each subsequent index has a lower or equal temperature. The core logic is: when processing a new temperature, before pushing its index onto the stack, we pop indices from the top while the new temperature is greater than the temperature at the top index. This ensures the decreasing property is preserved after the push. The intuition is that if the new temperature is higher than a temperature on the stack, that stacked temperature has found its warmer day (the new temperature), so we resolve it and remove it. What remains on the stack are temperatures that are still higher than or equal to the new temperature, maintaining the decreasing order. This property is crucial because it guarantees that when we later find a warmer temperature, it will be the first warmer day for all temperatures we pop. Time complexity for maintaining this property is O(1) amortized per element. Connection to L3: This builds on the concept of stack operations.

connectsTo.reason: This logic enables the popping subroutine

commonMistakes: not popping all eligible indices (breaking monotonic property)
or popping when temperatures are equal (should not pop as it's not warmer). The pseudocode pattern: while stack not empty
temperatures[current_index] > temperatures[stack[-1]]: pop
resolve; then push current_index

examples.input: Processing index 5 with temperature 72, current stack: [2,3,4] (temps: 75,71,69)
examples.trace:
Start: stack=[2,3,4] with temps [75,71,69], current temp=72 at index5.
Check top index=4, temp=69. 72 > 69 → pop 4. Stack=[2,3].
Check top index=3, temp=71. 72 > 71 → pop 3. Stack=[2].
Check top index=2, temp=75. 72 > 75 → false. Stop popping.
Push index5 onto stack. Stack=[2,5]. Now temps: index2=75, index5=72 → decreasing order (75 > 72).

examples.input: Processing index 3 with temperature 60, current stack: [0,1,2] (temps: 30,40,50)
examples.trace:
Start: stack=[0,1,2] with temps [30,40,50], current temp=60 at index3.
Check top index=2, temp=50. 60 > 50 → pop 2. Stack=[0,1].
Check top index=1, temp=40. 60 > 40 → pop 1. Stack=[0].
Check top index=0, temp=30. 60 > 30 → pop 0. Stack=[].
Push index3 onto stack. Stack=[3]. Now only temp 60 → trivially decreasing.

testCases.input: Processing index 1 with temperature 40, current stack: [0] (temp: 30)
Processing index 2 with temperature 50, current stack: [1] (temp: 40)
Processing index 3 with temperature 70, current stack: [0,1,2] (temps: 90,80,70)
Processing index 4 with temperature 65, current stack: [0,2,3] (temps: 85,80,85)

Processing index 5 with temperature 100, current stack: [3,4] (temps: 52,53)
Processing index 2 with temperature 50, current stack: [0] (temp: 50)
Processing index 3 with temperature 80, current stack: [0,1,2] (temps: 70,75,78)
Processing index 1 with temperature 60, current stack: [0] (temp: 70)
Processing index 4 with temperature 90, current stack: [0,1,2,3] (temps: 100,99,98,97)

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Operations for Index Storage
ladders.desc: Using a stack data structure to store indices of array elements while processing.

explanation.prose: A stack is a LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) data structure that supports push (add to top) and pop (remove from top) operations. In this problem, we use a stack to store indices of temperatures that haven't yet found a warmer day. The key insight is that by storing indices rather than temperatures, we can access both the index (for computing day differences) and the temperature (via array lookup). The stack helps manage which days are still 'waiting' for a warmer temperature. When we process a new day, we compare its temperature with temperatures at indices on the stack (starting from the top). This is efficient because we only need to check the top due to the monotonic property. The stack operations are: push(current_index) to add a new waiting day, and pop() to remove a day when its warmer day is found. We also need peek/top operation to check the top index without popping. Time complexity for each operation is O(1). Connection to L4: This builds on basic stack operations.

connectsTo.reason: This stack is used to maintain monotonic order

commonMistakes: using a stack of temperatures instead of indices (losing index information)
or not handling empty stack cases. The pseudocode pattern: stack = []; push: stack.append(index); pop: index = stack.pop(); peek: stack[-1] if stack not empty

examples.input: Initial stack: [], operations: push(0), push(1), pop(), push(2), peek()
examples.trace:
Start stack = [].
Push 0: stack.append(0) → stack = [0].
Push 1: stack.append(1) → stack = [0,1].
Pop: remove top → stack.pop() returns 1, stack = [0].
Push 2: stack.append(2) → stack = [0,2].
Peek: stack[-1] = 2. Final stack = [0,2], top index = 2.

examples.input: Initial stack: [3,4,5], operations: pop(), peek(), push(6), pop()
examples.trace:
Start stack = [3,4,5].
Pop: remove top → stack.pop() returns 5, stack = [3,4].
Peek: stack[-1] = 4.
Push 6: stack.append(6) → stack = [3,4,6].
Pop: remove top → stack.pop() returns 6, stack = [3,4]. Actually, let's correct: after push(6), stack is [3,4,6]. Pop returns 6, stack = [3,4]. So popped indices are 5 and 6. Final stack = [3,4].

testCases.input: Initial stack: [], operations: push(0), push(1), push(2)
Initial stack: [5], operations: peek(), push(6), peek()
Initial stack: [1,2,3], operations: pop(), push(4), pop()
Initial stack: [], operations: push(100), pop(), push(200)
Initial stack: [0,1], operations: peek(), pop(), peek()
Initial stack: [7,8,9], operations: pop(), pop(), push(10)
Initial stack: [3], operations: push(4), push(5), pop()
Initial stack: [6,7], operations: peek(), push(8), peek()
Initial stack: [1,2,3,4,5], operations: pop(), pop(), pop(), push(6)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Array Elements with Stack Top
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of comparing a current array element with the element at the index stored on top of the stack.

explanation.prose: This basic operation involves two steps: first, accessing the temperature at the current index in the array; second, if the stack is not empty, accessing the temperature at the index stored on top of the stack, and comparing the two values. Specifically, we check if the current temperature is greater than the temperature at the top index. This comparison drives the entire algorithm: if true, we trigger the popping subroutine; if false or stack empty, we push the current index. The operation requires careful handling of indices: we use stack[-1] to get the top index, then use that index to access temperatures[stack[-1]]. Connection to L5: This builds on array indexing and comparisons.

connectsTo.reason: This comparison is used in stack operations

commonMistakes: comparing indices instead of temperatures
or not checking if stack is empty before accessing top. The pseudocode pattern: if stack
temperatures[current_index] > temperatures[stack[-1]]: then pop
resolve; else: push current_index

examples.input: Current index: 1, temperature: 74, stack: [0] (top index 0 has temperature 73)
examples.trace:
Given temperatures array: index0=73, index1=74.
Stack = [0], so top index = 0.
Temperature at top index: temperatures[0] = 73.
Current temperature: temperatures[1] = 74.
Compare: 74 > 73 → true.

examples.input: Current index: 4, temperature: 69, stack: [2,3] (top index 3 has temperature 71)
examples.trace:
Given temperatures array: index2=75, index3=71, index4=69.
Stack = [2,3], so top index = 3.
Temperature at top index: temperatures[3] = 71.
Current temperature: temperatures[4] = 69.
Compare: 69 > 71 → false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Value Comparison
ladders.desc: The basic skill of accessing array elements by index and comparing numerical values.

explanation.prose: This foundational concept involves two parts: first, understanding that an array is a collection of elements accessible by indices (positions), typically starting at index 0. For example, in array temperatures = [73, 74, 75], temperatures[0] gives 73, temperatures[1] gives 74, etc. Second, comparing two numerical values using greater-than (>) operator to determine which is larger. In programming, we often access an element from one index and compare it with an element from another index. For instance, if we have index i and index j, we compare temperatures[i] and temperatures[j]. The result is a boolean: true if the first is greater, false otherwise. This is essential for the Daily Temperatures problem because we need to compare temperatures of different days. Time and space complexity are O(1) for these operations.

connectsTo.reason: This is used in the comparison operation

commonMistakes: off-by-one errors in indices
confusing indices with values
or incorrect comparison logic. Practice with simple arrays
comparisons builds intuition for the algorithm

examples.trace:
Array indices: 0 → 10, 1 → 20, 2 → 30.
temperatures[0] = 10.
temperatures[1] = 20.
Compare: 10 > 20 → false.

Array indices: 0 → 50, 1 → 40, 2 → 60.
temperatures[2] = 60.
temperatures[0] = 50.
Compare: 60 > 50 → true.

approaches.name: Array Backward Traversal O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate from the end to the start, using an auxiliary array to store the next warmer day's index. This approach skips days by jumping to known warmer indices, optimizing the search.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Daily Temperatures with Array Backward Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete solution using backward traversal with index jumping optimization

explanation.prose: This approach solves the Daily Temperatures problem by iterating from the last day to the first, using an auxiliary array to store the index of the next warmer day for each position. The key insight is that we can skip unnecessary comparisons by jumping to known warmer indices. Starting from the end, for each day i, we look at day j = i+1. If temperatures[j] > temperatures[i], we found the next warmer day. Otherwise, we check if result[j] is 0 (meaning no warmer day exists after j) - if so, we know no warmer day exists after i either. If result[j] is not 0, we jump to j + result[j] and continue checking. This jumping mechanism allows us to skip over days that we already know won't be warmer than our current day. The algorithm maintains O(n) time complexity because each day is visited at most twice - once when processing it, and potentially once when being jumped over. This approach is elegant because it leverages previously computed information to avoid redundant work, similar to how dynamic programming builds solutions from subproblems. The backward direction is crucial because it ensures that when we process day i, we already have information about all days after i.
commonMistakes: forgetting to handle the case where no warmer day exists (result should be

, incorrect index bounds when jumping, and not properly initializing the result array. The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the complete jumping logic described in the key subroutine

ladders.title: Key Subroutine — Index Jumping for Next Warmer Day
ladders.desc: Finding the next warmer day by jumping through previously computed indices

explanation.prose: This subroutine implements the core optimization of the backward traversal approach. Given a current day index i and the result array containing distances to warmer days for indices after i, we need to find the next warmer day efficiently. Instead of checking each subsequent day linearly (which would be O(n) per day), we use the result array to jump ahead. We start with j = i+1. While j is within bounds: if temperatures[j] > temperatures[i], we found our warmer day and return j-i. Otherwise, if result[j] == 0, there's no warmer day after j, so there can't be one after i either - return 0. If result[j] != 0, we jump to j = j + result[j] and continue. This works because result[j] tells us how many days to skip to reach a warmer temperature than day j. If day j is not warmer than day i, then any day that's not warmer than day j (which we skip over using result[j]) also won't be warmer than day i (since temperatures[i] >= temperatures[j] in this branch). The intuition is similar to path compression in union-find: we're building shortcuts to warmer temperatures. Time complexity for this subroutine is amortized O(1) per call because each jump moves us closer to a solution, and each index is visited at most twice.

connectsTo.reason: as it's the heart of the full solution, and to L2 as it implements the jumping technique that makes the algorithm efficient

commonMistakes: infinite loops when result[j] is 0 but we don't break
off-by-one errors in distance calculation
not handling the case where we jump beyond array bounds

examples.trace: i=3 (71), result=[?,?,?,0,0,0,0,0]. Start j=4 (69): 69 <= 71, result[4]=0 → no warmer after 4, so no warmer after 3. Wait - incorrect! Actually at this point result[4] might not be 0 yet since we process backward. Let's trace properly: We're at i=3, processing backward so result[4..7] are already computed. Assume result=[?,?,?,?,1,1,0,0]. j=4 (69): 69 <= 71, result[4]=1 so jump to j=5 (72): 72 > 71, distance = 5-3=2. Return 2.
